canto_title: The Divine Intervention and Dante's Rebirth of Will

scenes.summary: As day fades, Dante invokes the Muses and his high intellect to assist him in recounting his journey.
Dante acknowledges Virgil's noble ancestry and the historical significance of his descent from Aeneas.
Dante expresses his unworthiness for such a journey, feeling himself unfit to follow in the footsteps of great heroes.
The narrator describes how his own indecision and loss of purpose stalled his spiritual progress.
Virgil responds to Dante's hesitation by identifying his fear as a form of spiritual cowardice.
Virgil describes being among the uncommitted souls when a beautiful woman appeared to him.
Beatrice speaks with an angelic voice, commanding Virgil to guide Dante to safety.
After a pause, Dante asks Beatrice why she does not descend herself to assist him.
Beatrice explains that she is not subject to earthly fears or the pains of the Inferno because of her heavenly state.
Virgil recounts how Lucia prompted Beatrice to act, which compelled Beatrice to descend to the Limbo.
Upon hearing this, Virgil is moved to tears, which hastens the movement of the journey.
Virgil confronts Dante, questioning why he remains so hesitant despite the divine support surrounding him.
Dante uses a metaphor of flowers opening to the sun to describe his sudden restoration of courage.
Dante thanks the divine messengers and accepts Virgil as his leader and teacher.
With his will restored, Dante begins his ascent into the upward path of the forest.
